name: Lion's Mane
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated for most users.

sources.title: Neurotrophic and Neuroprotective Effects of Hericium erinaceus
Lion's Mane Mushroom (Hericium erinaceus): A Neuroprotective Fungus with Antioxidant, Anti-Inflammatory, and Antimicrobial Potential-A Narrative Review
Improvement of cognitive functions by oral intake of Hericium erinaceus
Acute effects of Guayusa tea and Nordic Lion’s Mane extracts
sources.citation: Single 1 g Nordic-grown Lion’s Mane extract; assessed 1–2 hours post-ingestion

sources.title: Acute effects of Hericium erinaceus extract on cognition and mood
sources.citation: 3 g 10:1 fruiting-body extract; acute; 18 healthy young adults; mostly null global outcomes

sources.title: Use of Hericium erinaceus as a potential therapeutic of mental disorders
Benefits, side effects, and uses of Hericium erinaceus as a supplement
sources.citation: Potential side effects include stomach discomfort, headache, allergic reactions; safety reporting limited

sources.title: Neuroprotective and Cognitive Benefits of Hericium erinaceus
sources.citation: 4 key studies; rare adverse effects; mild GI discomfort; one study 14.3% withdrawal due to side effects

sources.title: Acute and Chronic Effects of Lion's Mane Mushroom Supplementation

claimMap.claim: A 12-week human trial found MMSE improvement with Lion's Mane, but other cognitive tests were less clearly positive.
claimMap.notes: A 12-week human trial found MMSE improvement with Lion's Mane, but other cognitive tests were less clearly positive.

qualifiers.direction: positive on MMSE only; mixed tests

claimMap.claim: Lion's Mane showed tentative speed-of-performance and stress signals in healthy young adults, but the small pilot design limits confidence.
claimMap.notes: Lion's Mane showed tentative speed-of-performance and stress signals in healthy young adults, but the small pilot design limits confidence.

claimMap.claim: Lion's Mane has limited human RCT evidence suggesting possible cognitive support, but mood/anxiety evidence is not convincing.
claimMap.notes: Lion's Mane has limited human RCT evidence suggesting possible cognitive support, but mood/anxiety evidence is not convincing.

qualifiers.direction: positive for cognitive impairment; not for depression/anxiety
qualifiers.dose_or_duration: At least 12 weeks may be needed per review conclusion

claimMap.claim: A single 3 g dose of 10:1 Lion’s Mane fruiting-body extract did not improve global cognition/mood, though one task-specific signal was observed.
claimMap.notes: A single 3 g dose of 10:1 Lion’s Mane fruiting-body extract did not improve global cognition/mood, though one task-specific signal was observed.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Single acute dose: 3 g of 10:1 Hericium erinaceus fruiting body extract; assessed 90 minutes post-dose

claimMap.claim: Lion’s Mane at 1.8 g/day for 28 days has only tentative pilot evidence for speed of performance and stress trends in healthy young adults.
claimMap.notes: Lion’s Mane at 1.8 g/day for 28 days has only tentative pilot evidence for speed of performance and stress trends in healthy young adults.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Hericium erinaceus 1.8 g/day; acute 60-minute assessment and chronic 28-day intervention

claimMap.claim: Lion's Mane should be framed as generally food-like but not risk-free; potential side effects include stomach discomfort, headache, and allergic reactions.
claimMap.notes: Lion's Mane should be framed as generally food-like but not risk-free; potential side effects include stomach discomfort, headache, and allergic reactions.

qualifiers.direction: limited safety reporting; mild side effects possible

claimMap.claim: Clinical studies report rare adverse effects, mostly mild GI discomfort, but long-term safety and efficacy need larger trials.
claimMap.notes: Clinical studies report rare adverse effects, mostly mild GI discomfort, but long-term safety and efficacy need larger trials.

qualifiers.population: Recent clinical studies, participants 31-68 per study

claimMap.claim: A single 1 g Nordic-grown Lion’s Mane extract dose showed task-specific acute cognitive effects in one crossover study.
claimMap.notes: A single 1 g Nordic-grown Lion’s Mane extract dose showed task-specific acute cognitive effects in one crossover study.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Single dose: 1 g Nordic-grown Lion’s Mane extract; assessed at 1 and 2 hours post-ingestion
qualifiers.population: Adults in acute cognition testing study

dosage: Human studies vary: 1 g Nordic-grown extract acutely, 1.8 g/day for 28 days, and 3 g of 10:1 fruiting-body extract acutely. Older cognitive-impairment trials need separate extraction before use. | Fruiting-body extract; Nordic-grown extract; 10:1 fruiting-body extract. Mycelium/erinacine claims should be separate from fruiting-body human evidence. | Do not merge fruiting body, mycelium, erinacine-enriched products, culinary mushroom, and 10:1 extract as equivalent.
typical_dosage: Human studies vary: 1 g Nordic-grown extract acutely, 1.8 g/day for 28 days, and 3 g of 10:1 fruiting-body extract acutely. Older cognitive-impairment trials need separate extraction before use. | Fruiting-body extract; Nordic-grown extract; 10:1 fruiting-body extract. Mycelium/erinacine claims should be separate from fruiting-body human evidence. | Do not merge fruiting body, mycelium, erinacine-enriched products, culinary mushroom, and 10:1 extract as equivalent.
